title: Quiz: Classical Fraud Detection Methods
description: Test your understanding of traditional fraud detection approaches

questions.question: Why is accuracy a poor metric for fraud detection?

options.text: Accuracy is too difficult to calculate
With rare fraud (<1%), predicting all as legitimate gives 99%+ accuracy

questions.explanation: When fraud is rare (e.g., 0.1%), a model that predicts every transaction as legitimate achieves 99.9% accuracy while catching zero fraud. This makes accuracy misleading for imbalanced classification problems.

questions.question: What does SMOTE do to address class imbalance?

options.text: Duplicates exact copies of minority class samples
Creates synthetic samples by interpolating between minority class neighbors

questions.explanation: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) creates new synthetic samples by interpolating between existing minority class samples and their k nearest neighbors, generating diverse training examples.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of rule-based fraud detection?

options.text: Ability to detect all fraud patterns

questions.explanation: Rule-based systems are highly interpretable (you can explain exactly why a transaction was flagged) and very fast to execute. However, they struggle with novel fraud patterns that weren't anticipated in the rules.

questions.question: What is concept drift in fraud detection?

options.text: When fraudsters adapt tactics, making old patterns obsolete

questions.explanation: Concept drift occurs when fraudsters change their tactics over time, making previously effective detection patterns obsolete. This requires continuous model monitoring and retraining.

questions.question: In fraud detection, a false positive (flagging a legitimate transaction as fraud) is typically more costly than a false negative (missing actual fraud).
questions.explanation: False. Missing actual fraud (false negative) is typically much more costly than a false positive. A missed fraud might cost hundreds of dollars in losses, while a false positive mainly causes customer friction and investigation costs.

questions.question: What does Precision@K measure in fraud detection?

options.text: The precision of the top K features
The percentage of true frauds among the K highest-scored transactions
The precision after K iterations of training

questions.explanation: Precision@K measures what fraction of the top K highest-scored (most suspicious) transactions are actually fraud. This is practical when investigators can only review a limited number of alerts.

questions.question: Which resampling technique risks overfitting by creating exact copies of samples?
questions.explanation: Random oversampling creates exact copies of minority class samples, which can lead to overfitting as the model may memorize specific fraud examples rather than learning general patterns. SMOTE addresses this by creating synthetic variations.

questions.question: Which are important metrics for evaluating fraud detection models? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Precision (how many flags are actual fraud)

questions.explanation: Recall, precision, and AUC-ROC are critical metrics for fraud detection. Recall measures fraud catch rate, precision measures alert quality, and AUC evaluates overall model discrimination. Training time is less important than detection quality.
